Most arguments against pickling (java serialization, etc.) center around the fact that it's a bad format for shipping or persisting data, but Dataflow uses it to ship (references to) "code" around (e.g. a users particular subclass of DoFn) to be executed on remote machines. 

The difficulty here is that a user's DoFn may hold a datastore client object preventing us from serializing their Fn. One could of course force authors of such DoFns to implement `__setstate__`/`__getstate__` to serialize and re-instanciate the client object, but that would place the burden on every user rather than the library.

So, it looks like this is still an issue, in that touching a file in the root of the repository will invalidate the listing there, which will invalidate all `Snapshots`, because they depend on the filesystem operations. Basically everything else in the graph depends on a `Snapshot`, so the result is that although only one new IO happens, we end up re-constructing the `Snapshot`s (which requires some round trips to the database) and all rule logic re-runs.

Fixing #4558 seems like the least contorted way to resolve this, but will re-open this until we resolve #4558.

You can still use Parameters will default values today and !Ref the parameter in your Runtime. You have to write a few extra redundant lines for the first time you write the template.

But yes, your proposal keeps the template a bit more terse. One could view the global defaults as a system-configuration for their entire app. But then, SAM has to make assumptions about name of the parameter used by global defaults, which is bad. 

When you override defaults, now your template suddenly has an implicit if-then-else logic which isn't explicitly written down. It makes the template harder to reason about.

This is certainly an interesting proposal, but for sake of simplicity I would vote against it.

`AWS::Serverless::Function` can take multiple [Managed Policy names](https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/master/versions/2016-10-31.md#awsserverlessfunction). Like this:

```
Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
Properties:
    Runtime: nodejs4.3
    Policies:
        - AWSS3FullAccess
        - AWSDynamoDBFullAccess
```
 SAM will automatically add LambdaBasicExecution role to the permissions.
 
Above will work for AWS Managed Policies. If you are attaching your own Custom Managed policy, you need to follow @heitorlessa's example above. Create your own IAM role and attach the role to SAM function

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import soco
import logging
import time
import threading

players = soco.discover()

logging.basicConfig()
logging.getLogger('soco.events').setLevel(logging.INFO)

counter = 0
class Queue():
    def put(self, item, block=True, timeout=None):
        global counter
        counter = counter+1

def work():
    x = 0
    while x < 1000000:
        x = x+1    

def subscribe(player):
    sub = player.avTransport.subscribe(event_queue=Queue())
    time.sleep(2)
    sub.unsubscribe()

for _ in range(0, 10):
    threading.Thread(target=work).start()

threads = []
for player in players:
    threads.append(threading.Thread(target=subscribe, args=(player,)))
[t.start() for t in threads]
[t.join() for t in threads]

print(counter)
```

One path to implementation would be to extend `ChannelMetadataCache` with a date field like `datetime_last_updated`.

* When a channel is import from the internet or local drive, then `datetime_last_updated` is equal to the time when the download is completed.
* When a channel is "imported" from `.kolibri` folder and does not already exist in the cache, then `datetime_last_updated` is equal to the current datetime.
* When a channel is "imported" from `.kolibri` folder and already exists in the cache, then its `datetime_last_updated` is left alone.

It started from a mix of AWS best practices and my own research, but now it's a mix of many things.

Whenever I need to look into a new service, I do my own research (go through best practices, benchmarks, ...) and also experience with as many settings as I can myself. I find that going through the process of creating resources is a good way to identify security-related settings. So far, I think this has been the best way for me to come up with new test cases.

I'm not a member of the Cloud Security Alliance so I've never looked at their resources. I've looked at the CIS benchmark as well and it's an overall pretty good source of truth (except for the monitoring section as this may be implemented in many ways without cloudwatch). I tried implementing as many checks as I could (from v1) in https://github.com/nccgroup/Scout2/blob/master/AWSScout2/rules/data/rulesets/cis-02-29-2016.json and will probably try to go through the latest version to create additional checks.

For the default ruleset, I try to find the right compromise between breadth of coverage and a limited number of false positives. There are a number of optional rules that may be enabled for specific use cases or tight controls (for example integration with managed IP ranges, documented there : https://blog.l01cd3v.fr/aws/2015/11/17/ip_ranges_management.html).

I tried using this and hit a couple of problems.

Sometimes the config is aws_external_id some as external_id. Awscli uses the latter, but scout and anything else that uses opinel uses the former. 
(If you do get a working configuration, the temporary credentials can be cached by awscli. This is good behaviour, but can be confusing for two consecutive tests.)
 Scout2 has some code to parse cross account roles and it does work but it is not compatible with the awscli.

in ~/.aws/config I have
```
[yyy]
region=us-west-2
role_arn=arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/cross_role_test
external_id=666
aws_external_id=666
source_profile=xxx
```

and in ~/.aws/credentials I have
```
[xxx]
region=us-west-2
aws_access_key_id=AIHFBAFHBAIHBFIAHFBIA
aws_secret_access_key=aljehrbfaiebrfiaebrfipebr

[zzz]
region=us-west-2
aws_access_key_id=AIHFBAFHBAIHBFIAHFBIA
aws_secret_access_key=aljehrbfaiebrfiaebrfipebr
region=us-west-2
role_arn=arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/cross_role_test
external_id=666
source_profile=zzz
```

So the following commands work
```
aws --profile=zzz  ec2 describe-instances —debug
Scout2 --no-browser --force --services ec2 --profile yyy
```
but
`Scout2 --no-browser --force --services ec2 —profile zzz`
goes into a stack death loop and
`aws --profile=yyy  ec2 describe-instances —debug`
doesn’t try the role.
So it is possible to write a config for scout2 but it is incompatible with one for awscli.

Probably a worse issue is that we have no way of passing in these parameters in the environment.
To use this from a script we would need to go back to overwriting the config for each set of credentials, which is not convenient.

I reported a ticket in opinel (https://github.com/nccgroup/opinel/issues/26) and fix in https://github.com/nccgroup/opinel/pull/27.

Currently all the travis tests fail, but I don’t think this is because of my code change.

The existing gas estimation test now covers the original problem with the transaction nonce: mixing the actual sender nonce with the dummy address.

These lines in the original do_call method made the non-zero nonce valid:

```
                if not hasattr(_transaction, "get_sender"):
                    _transaction.get_sender = \ 
                        lambda: to_bytes(hexstr=DEFAULT_DO_CALL_SENDER)
                    _transaction.sender = to_bytes(hexstr=DEFAULT_DO_CALL_SENDER)
```

The conditional has been removed so the sender is always replaced with the dummy address. Now the case is caught, validation will fail with the dummy sender mixed with the original sender nonce.

There's a lot of history here.  It starts with a hard requirement the metadata for a package needs to evolve over time, most prominently because upper bounds need to get added to the packages dependency version constraints over time.  This unknowable information at package build time.

What is the source of truth for package metadata?  Is it repodata.json or within the package itself?  We've at this point settled on the source of truth being the metadata contained within the package itself.  There are multiple reasons that's preferable.  One is that it let's `conda index` build repodata.json, as most users expect.  It also ensures that repodata as known by anaconda.org is correct with `anaconda upload`.

Ilan over the last couple months has thus gone through repo.continuum.io and updated metadata within packages.

At some point, the ultimate solution to all of these problems will probably be a rider file alongside the package tarball that contains information that can't be included in the tarball itself (md5, signature, etc) along with overrides to metadata content.  There's probably a good deal of planning and work to build that facility out properly though.
Thanks.

@ijstokes thank you for documenting these issues, it's very helpful. I hope this is a suitable place to describe another multi-user setup.

At our institute we are trying to provide a multi-user Anaconda installation to give (a handful of advanced) users flexible access to scipy an related libraries. We are trying to avoid users running environments in their home folders in an effort to enhance transparency and reproducability.

Our current setup involves a root environment controlled by the admin, and a root condarc file that uses `envs_dirs:` to specify a shared workspace for environments. This would also make it easy to copy/clone other users environments and allow multiple users to connect to the same environment (with appropriate cation). We also have a jupyterhub instance that users can connect their environments to via a kernelspec.

This set up was working well until I noticed that several environments had broken/missing packages that went undetected because [issue 2437](https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/2437). I'm investigating upgrading conda from 4.0.5 to 4.2.x to avoid the silently failing hard-links, but the underlying permissions issues still persist. 

I'm curious if enforcing group read/write access (at the file system level) to the shared .pkgs directory will fix the permissions issues and/or result in other issues?

One more thing:
> Anaconda "free thing you download" is targeted for individual use, and optimized around that. There are many complexities involved in addressing deplopyments of "Anaconda thing-you-download" in a multi-user environment and we may only implement the necessary capabilities in our commercial Anaconda Enterprise product.

If multi-user environments are not going to be supported outside of enterprise, it would be very helpful to make this clear in the documentation for a [centralized installation](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/install/central.html). It was this documentation that lead me down the road of a shared installation.

Thanks, Tim.

I feel the changes from this PR are (by designed/necessity) incomplete as they only fix the `(source|conda) (activate|deactivate)` case but nothing more, e.g., with those changes,
```bash
echo raise >io.py
conda activate
conda install foo
```
will successfully activate but fail to install.
Regarding the entry point for `python -m conda`, I don't think this is much relevant for the user as they are unlikely to not use `conda` instead -- and if `python -m conda` is used in development/CI systems, it doesn't really matter as there you could/should easily avoid thinks like `touch io.py && conda ...`.
I understand your reasoning (esp. "setuptools/pkg_resources shenanigans" 😑...), but I still think the modified console script I proposed is the more appropriate and consistent fix.

Here's what I'm seeing:
- `iteritems` and `iterkeys` from `conda.compat` are easily removed.
- `url_path` from `conda.utils` can be duplicated.
- `pkgs_dirs`: this is needed by the `package_cache` function. `install.main` can populate that value if it is needed. So we can try to import it from conda.config; if that fails we set it to the empty list and let `main` populate that list.
- `root_dir`: this is used by `move_path_to_trash`, which the standalone installer doesn't need.
That leaves `url_channel`. That one will be a little complicated but I believe we can replace it with a sensible default.

My understanding of this being fixed in node is that I believe node and npm themselves no longer have a problem with extremely deep paths on Windows, and continue to routinely make paths greater than 260 characters and that won't change. There was an improvement in path length when npm started deduping packages by default, resulting in a slightly reduced path length, but then shortly after it also increased its dependencies essentially negating that improvement. That was always a partial fix that would only work for some packages.

It's AOK for me if this starts out as a documentation I can point to, even if it's a manual fix requiring admin permissions for some folks. Of course, true support for long paths would be ideal, but a clear "You need to do this..." link would be a great start. Double bonus if it could be in the error message from conda itself when install fails due to long path errors.

>An underscore should be treated the same as a . for ordering purposes.

So why don't you just use . if they are the same anyway? The underscore character is very precious (the only non-letter treated as a letter in virtually all languages) and shouldn't be wasted for redundant functionality. FWIW, since the R language uses $ as separator between objects and members (foo.bar in Python becomes foo$bar in R), this would be a much better choice if an additional separator were really needed.

From the point of view of the VersionOrder class, the error message is actually correct: the version string `.post` has an empty version component before the dot, which is illegal. A valid version string would be something like `1.post` or `1.2.3post1`. One could easily change this rule (e.g. by interpreting empty components as zeros), but then a version like `...` (only dots) would become legal (it would be interpreted as `0.0.0.0`). I'm sceptical if this is desirable.

The interesting question is *why* the version string `.post` was passed to VersionOrder. There are two possibilities: Either, channel danielballan actually contains a package with an illegal version number. Or conda has a bug in version string parsing that causes part of the version string to be eaten away.

I am primarily a Python, C/C++ and Qt developer. I wrote OpenCobolIDE years
ago when I was attempting some COBOL lessons at school and could not find a
good IDE to work with on GNU/Linux. Nowadays I don't even work with COBOL but
keep maintaining the IDE because I like the language and it's a good showcase
of what you can do with pyQode.  

Two years ago I've started to work on a python IDE. I quickly realised that
the codebase was very similar to OCIDE and it would take me too much time to
maintain both so I started to work on HackEdit (keep in mind I am doing all
this during my freetime, as a hobby project).

  

The ultimate goal of HackEdit is that it should allow the user to turn it into
a language/technology specific IDE (i.e. GnuCOBOL integration should be as
good as in OCIDE).

  

You shouldn't be afraid or sorry, HackEdit + the cobol plugins give you an
even better IDE than OCIDE, because HE already have all the generic features
you expect from an IDE (searching through all the project files, goto dialog
like in Sublime text, external tools, vcs integration,...) and the COBOL
plugins provides all the features you can find in OCIDE (and even more, i.e.
you can configure own pre-parsers, there will be a debugger,...).

  

You can really consider HackEdit (with the COBOL plugins) as the  next major
version of OCIDE. You won't loose any feature!

  

I know at least one OCIDE user who already made the switch to HE
(http://sourceforge.net/p/open-cobol/discussion/help/thread/e42d9701/#801d).

  

At the moment, the main drawbacks of HE are that there is no windows installer
and there are shortcomings with file indexing (especially for big projects,
should be ok for small projects). Those points should be fixed for the beta
release. I encourage you to try out HackEdit (open an issue on the HE tracker
if you have trouble installing it).

Does the reverse:True also work ? (the descriptions states : "This means that if all index names share the same pattern with a date—e.g. index-2016.03.01—older indices will be selected first." However, I don't know if older indices are **selected** to compute the aggregated size of the indices first (in that case it will not be selected by the filter), or are **selected** first by the filter (meaning it will begin adding indices size on the opposite order). It it the second option for me (so the following should work).
```
- filtertype: space
      disk_space: 20
      reverse: True
      use_age: 
      source:
      timestring:
      field:
      stats_result:
```

The two last solutions could even be better ideas, if we consider that maybe some indices have been created at a different moment compated to what is stored in its name: maybe an old index containing corrupted data was deleted and created/filled again quite recently.

curator show indices --older-than 7 --timestring %Y.%m.%d --time-unit days --prefix logstash

https://github.com/Azure/azure-iot-cli-extension/blob/dev/azext_iot/operations/events3/_events.py#L111
to:
`results = await asyncio.gather(*coroutines, return_exceptions=True)`

Then you can chose to act on the results array as you see fit - though it's worth noting that this array will often not return the asyncio.Cancellation or KeybaordInterrupt errors. In most of my tests the results were all None. But still - it seems to handle the traceback.

I've managed to reduce the hanging scenarios significantly - but there's still one area in which I can occasionally reproduce a freeze that I'm struggling to isolate.

But still, progresssssssssssss :)

> under which conditions would files and exclude take effect? or would they be anded with the types?

My thinking is the AND them. The list of objects to consider running hooks on is built as it is currently (no change; all objects that match the `files` regex but don't match the `exclude` regex), then we filter this list to only contain objects that have at least one tag in `types`.

In this way, hooks are entirely backwards compatible (except for symlinks/submodules, assuming the `types` default is `['file']`).

I think this is also the most obvious from a developer's perspective. For `files` they specify whatever paths they want hooks to run under (e.g. `files: ^src/`), for exclude they specify paths to exclude, and they can let the hook worry about specific extensions/types. Currently to only check some specific paths they have to either list all others in `excludes` one-by-one, duplicate the logic in the `hooks.yaml`, or use negative lookaheads in `exclude` like above (which most, including me, don't understand).

Hey @zonca that's great to hear!

Here's how I'd recommend doing it, though if other people have different suggestions we can certainly discuss this further. I think the idea of having a global "enrollment" system is worthwhile, though the drawback is that it means individual instructors can't selectively edit the enrollment of their courses -- either you provide group-level access to all instructors, meaning any instructor can modify the enrollment for any course, or you provide access only to an admin who manages all enrollment information for all courses. So, with that caveat, here's how I envision this would work:

1. Create a database in the exchange directory, e.g. `/srv/nbgrader/exchange/enrollment.db`. This should be readable by everyone but only writeable by one person or one group, as described above.
2. Create a new config option for the `Exchange` class (in `nbgrader/exchange/exchange.py`) which is the name of the database.
3. Add a function to the `Exchange` class that takes the user id of a student and a course id and checks the database to see if the student is enrolled in the class.
4. Add another function to the `Exchange` class that takes the user id of a student and returns the list of classes they are enrolled in.
5. Modify the `ExchangeFetch` class to verify that the student is enrolled in the class before fetching.
6. Modify the `ExchangeSubmit` class to verify that the student is enrolled in the class before submitting.
7. Modify the `ExchangeList` class to only consider course ids that the student is enrolled in.

To manage the enrollment database, we probably want to create a new database-management API (analogous to the models and gradebook defined in `nbgrader/api.py`) and then new subcommands to import and edit the enrollments (analogous to the commands defined in `nbgrader/apps/dbapp.py`). The subcommands could maybe be a separate PR, though.

:+1: !

I haven't seen a good way to do this with the exchange directory, currently, due to the way the permissions on it work. In general I want to have a submission system that works without the exchange directory, making it more flexible for passing files between different users. So for now probably emailing is the right way to go.

In the feedback app, the `GetGrades` preprocessor extracts the comments and grades from the database and puts them in a dictionary, which ultimately get rendered into this template: https://github.com/jupyter/nbgrader/blob/master/nbgrader/formgrader/templates/feedback.tpl

To rebase and resolve merge conflicts, I usually:

- Ensure master is up to date - `git checkout master` and `git pull` (from jupyter/notebook, rather than your fork on Github)
- Check out this branch again - `git checkout jep16`
- Run `git rebase master`
- Each time it reaches a conflict, it will stop and tell you about it. Run `git status` to see which files have conflicts. Fix them in a text editor (look for markers like `>>>>>` and `<<<<<`). Then `git add` the files you've fixed, and run `git rebase --continue`.
- Once it has got through all the commits, it will stop without a message about conflicts.
- Then you need to force push the rebased branch - `git push -f <my_remote> jep16` (replace `<my_remote>` with the name it has for your Github fork of the repo.

Just to point out that you can do this in regular Python, without resorting to any extra magic:

Cell 1:
```python
class MyClass():
    def method1(self):
        print("method1")
```
Cell 2:
```python
class MyClass(MyClass):
    def method2(self):
        print("method2")
```
Cell 3:
```python
instance = MyClass()
instance.method1()
instance.method2()
```
That is, you can define a class recursively, cell by cell. I don't think the final class is any different from one defined all in one cell. So you don't need an extra package to "solve" this problem.

Also, I would tweak your `jacobian_vector_product` to do the caching thing we like now:

```python
def jacobian_vector_product(f):
    def jvp_maker(x):
        f_vjp, f_x = make_vjp(f)(x)
        f_vjp_vjp, _ = make_vjp(f_vjp)(np.zeros_like(f_x))
        return f_vjp_vjp  # f_vjp_vjp is just f_jvp!
    return jvp_maker
```

We are managing releases in PyPI, which is the preferred method of installing giraffez given that building the extensions from source (rather than installing the distributed wheels via pip) takes varying degrees of environment setup depending on platform. The intent is that "work in progress" is not committed to the master branch, so in that sense it is intentionally stable. 

Regarding API stability, we are following semantic versioning with giraffez. At the point of another major release in the future we may utilize tagging/GitHub releases to better delineate version changes -- at the moment it did not seem particularly useful to provide "releases" via GitHub, which would essentially be a snapshot of the project source from a particular commit. If there is a desire for that outside of PyPI, I would be open to it, but I haven't thought of the motivation for it yet. If you are using giraffez we highly recommend keeping current with the latest release, as the majority of patch/minor version increments are bugfixes and performance enhancements. Locking a particular version is easily accomplished via pip, but if another facility for doing so is needed I wouldn't rule out the possibility.

Checking the delimiter for every byte of data would introduce a very significant performance change to cover this case.  While I agree that it would be nice to find that the source data has a character that is being used for a delimiter, but I think for the most part this is why the `to_list` and `to_dict` exists so that in the case that this behavior is desired they can be used to produce those results with a higher level of safety (i.e. use `to_list` and pass to Python's `csv` module).  Since `to_str` is building a buffer in C and returning that as text it would be a fairly large change to try and introduce the kind of logic that may address some of these concerns like a quote character.  This was not originally in scope for this because `to_str` is suppose to be fast and straight-forward, and I also wish to discourage the use of character delimited formats altogether because they are not portable or safe to use in far too many cases, vs. something like JSON which fully supports unicode, is self-describing of all fields, and is portable regardless of OS/CPU Architecture.

I think it is possible to better abstract this so that it is either more apparent how to solve issues like in your example, or to just provide a better interface that would solve for this automatically.  For example, `to_str` is still available but under the hood it just calls `to_list` and uses something like `csv` rather than providing the other option that builds the string in C.  Thoughts?

```
$ sshuttle -D --pidfile=/var/run/sshuttle-$(date +%s).pid --remote ${SSH_PROXY_USER}@${SSH_PROXY_HOST}:${SSH_PROXY_PORT} ${REAL_SERVER}
```

Hope it helps!

We are directly uploading raw html to the organization wiki. Hence, `_generate_report` remains same, but we subclass `HTMLReport` and override its `_save_report` method accordingly.

Thanks for the contribution, but what are the benefits to switch over to `tox-travis` for `pytest-xdist`? I like `tox-travis` for projects with simple tox files (basically only `py27`, `py35`, etc test envs), which is not the case here. Using tox-travis here means that we cram more test runs into less jobs, which will probably increase the overall build time. Additionally, it creates an inconsistency to what we have for AppVeyor: having both travis and appveyor list the test envs in the same way is good consistency IMO. 

I'm just curious in case there are obvious benefits that I'm missing. 😁

That's how xdist works: all workers collect all tests, which are then sent to master. The master then is responsible to send tests ids for to each worker to run.

@mvantellingen Merged in master again. `TestScales.test_decimals` was broken. 

Basically, the weight attribute is a `float` (and there is no decimal type), so when the scale weighs 3 items that weight `0.3`, it ends up with `3 * Decimal(0.3)` which not the same as `Decimal('0.9')` due to float approximation. One solution is to quantize the decimal result with the `Decimal.quantize` method to round the weight to the nearest tenths (as I have done here). Another solution would be to use `self.assertAlmostEqual` instead, but I thought that was less clear.

As mentioned by @jforbess I think that one often uses a lat/lon different from the TMY data. So I am not really sure, if it is a good idea to return the `Location` from the TMY reader. This may be different for other data sources, e.g. measurement data or satellite data. 

A `System` or perhaps better a `PVSystem` class would be really nice. However I wouldn't derive it from `Location` because it is something completely different. And it is a very complicated task to develop something that covers all aspects. Here are some points, that come to my mind:

* Should be able to describe a whole PV system, e.g. components (inverters, modules, transformers, wiring, mounting, connection to strings, ...)
* The components should be characterized independent of the models used for the simulation of their behaviour (e.g. not parameters of the two diode model for a PV module, as there are other models, that use different parameters). Alternatively it should be possible to include parameters of multiple models.
* I don't know a standard for this, maybe because there is no standard. We could look at other software (e.g. PVSyst, SAM ...). There was the idea of `PVML`, a XML dialect dedicated to PV systems within the European project "Performance", but as far as I remember without published results.
* It should be able to be read from various sources and exported to different formats 

We cannot cover all of this in pvlib (maybe this would be a good topic for a PhD and I think it would be very useful to have a standard for the whole PV community). And up to now we don't need all of this (e.g. we don't have models for calculation of shading losses, wiring losses, transformers ...). But maybe we should have it on mind when we implement something like this.

And I agree, this is something important for our API, so we should have something quite stable for the future.
But IMO you _should_ switch to Python-3.6 for many other reasons, mainly because support for Python-2 ends in 2020, and many packages are already not supporting Python-2 anymore

Yes, this is common practice, see e.g.:

http://www.wolframalpha.com/input/?i=Series[1%2FCos[x%2FLog[x]],+{x,+0,+10}]

It would be nice to formalize that, but essentially log(x) grows slower than x, so this series makes sense. E.g. `x/log(x)` for small positive `x` is a beautiful function, going to zero (of course, for x=1 it's singular, but that's another issue).

Again, it would be nice to formalize this, but my intuition is telling me that this series is the best that you can do, since you can't expand `log(x)` around zero.

It is also used in the SymPy's limit algorithm.

This is subtle, but not an accident.
The example uses several multi-patterns, which can become quite expensive.
The default behavior of Z3 is to limit the instantiation of multi-patterns. 

z3 unk.smt2
unknown
(:reason-unknown (incomplete quantifiers))

You can overwrite the default by controlling parameters. 
One parameter that lets Z3 use (more) multi-patterns directly is called smt.qe.max_multi_patterns. 
There is also an internal parameter that isn't exposed that controls the number of rounds admitted for lazy pattern instantiation. It is set at 2. Increasing this number should allow Z3 to also find the match, but as it seems not really exposed at this point I would recommend trying the smt.qe.max_multi_patterns value. 

z3 unk.smt2 smt.qi.max_multi_patterns=3
unsat
(:reason-unknown ok)

I did some extra investigation and found a solution.

When java loads a dynamic library it only looks at the locations in java.library.path.
From https://developer.apple.com/library/content/qa/qa1170/_index.html
we find that the standard java.library.path contains :
                
          - ~/Library/Java/Extensions/ : for single user 
          - /Library/Java/Extensions   : for all users
          - $JAVA_HOME/lib/ext         : for specific jvm installation

Normally when an exectutable or library loads  a dynamic library it looks at location specified by the operating system.
From https://developer.apple.com/library/content/documentation/DeveloperTools/Conceptual/DynamicLibraries/100-Articles/UsingDynamicLibraries.html
we find that the standard locations where OS X looks are    
      
            ~/lib: for single user 
            /usr/local/lib: for all users 
            /usr/lib: contains system protected libraries by SIP   (run: /bin/ls -lOd /usr/lib )

Note that above locations are the locations set in the default value for DYLD_FALLBACK_LIBRARY_PATH. These locations are still used when SIP is enabled as I found by the following reference: 

    https://community.plm.automation.siemens.com/t5/3D-Simulation-Simcenter-3D-Forum/NX-Support-for-El-Capitan-MAC-OSX-v10-11/td-p/327539
    01-11-2016 
 
        As it turns out, although DYLD* variables are unavailable in El Capitan, the default value for
        DYLD_FALLBACK_LIBRARY_PATH is still set internally, and one directory in that path is /usr/local/lib.
             

So for z3 above markusvoelter concluded that it is not the initial loading of libz3java that fails, but rather,  the subsequent loading of libz3.dylib. Loading of the JNI library succeeds because markusvoelter set the java.library.path  explicitly on the commandline in its test.  However subsequent loading of  libz3.dylib by libz3java.dylib fails because:
 * java is a SIP protected executable which ignores user set DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH      (run: /bin/ls -lO $(which java) )   
 * libz3.dylib is not in one the standard locations of  OS X : not in ~/lib, /usr/local/lib, /usr/lib

So the quickfix is to move or link  libz3.dylib in ~/lib.

Personally I'm the only user of my macbook and prefer not to put libraries in my homedir, so I prefer the solution :
 - put  JNI dynamic link libraries in  /Library/Java/Extensions   e.g. libz3java.dylib
 - put none-JNI dynamic link libraries in /usr/local/lib          e.g. libz3.dylib 

Note: To preserve the original installation I prefer soft linking into the original installation which works also fine.
 
An alternative solution is to relink the subsequent library dependency of libz3.dylib in libz3java.dylib with an absolute path so that it doesn't need a PATH. For how to do this look at:  http://thecourtsofchaos.com/2013/09/16/how-to-copy-and-relink-binaries-on-osx/ 

However personally I prefer to just use the standard, that is use the above standard locations!!

> I feel like that would severely degrade the readability of the code though. And the most common use cases actually do not require it. It goes against 'Pragmatism' and 'Don't get in the way' a bit, imo.

Well, I think we could do it only adding about 5 or 10 lines of code to the `Layer` class.

> Personally I don't understand the issue with the name MergeLayer.

The `RecurrentLayer` isn't merging its input with the mask; it's using the mask to decide which entires in its input to use.  Purely semantically, "merge" makes it sound like it is consuming both inputs.   The `InputLayer` which supplies the mask would be re-used multiple times throughout the network's graph.  See @f0k's usage: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/337#issuecomment-124475829

> Would MultipleInputsLayer not be even more confusing? It still only accepts inputs from multiple layers, which is something different than accepting multiple inputs.

How is that different?

All this being said, if it's something you don't like, I don't think it's important enough for me to try to convince you to change your mind.  It's just awkward semantically for recurrent layers to be subclasses of a `MergeLayer` when they don't merge anything.

> I think it's a good idea to make the basic examples self-contained.

Yes, the basic example in the `Lasagne` repository should be self-contained and "flat". The notebooks in the `Recipes` repository may externalize things like (down)loading the data so they can be shared across recipes.

> How popular is CG right now for training neural nets? This is an honest question, I really have no idea. HF seems to have fallen out of favour lately, at least ...

CG isn't useful for nonlinear problems, so it would probably only be used within HF, or other to-be-invented optimizers. For HF, I guess one of the main problems is that it's terribly complicated to get right (there are some nifty details), and most things seem to work fine with SGD+Momentum anyway. If it was easy to try HF, more people might do it (even if it was just to discover that it doesn't give an edge over SGD with carefully-chosen initializations).

Yes, but it's using Theano only for computing the gradient and Rop, everything else happens on CPU in numpy, so it's limited in performance.

To test your FS object, you would do something like this...

```python
from fs.test import FSTestCases

class TestMyFS(FSTestCases):

        # return your FS interface

If you save that as `test_myfs.py` or similar, then run `nosetests -v` it will run the tests.

The naming is important, `nose` runs all the tests that start with the word `test`.

> -So, uwsgi is default right?I don't ask if uwsgi should be enabled or not?

2

All good points:

- It's not clear to me that featurize_many should natively support pd DataFrame or Series in the first place. 

I have found I use Pandas as inputs as `featurize_many` somewhat frequently. For all its benefits, employing `featurize_dataframe` results in me needing to keep track of the feature labels. E.g., 

```python
data = f.featurize_dataframe(data, 'composition')
model.fit(data[f.feature_labels()], f['energy`])
```

which is just verbose enough to make it common for me to just get the numpy array with the features

```python
X = f.featurize_many(data['composition'])
model.fit(X, data['energy'])
```

The latter snippet works 90% of the time, except for when there is no entry with an index of `0`. (Hence my motivation for making this PR) 

- What if there is some other matrix-like object but not quite a matrix that people use, do we need to support that too?

I would limit special cases to Pandas, as many components of matminer are designed to be Pandas-friendly.

- If you really do think it's useful to add pd.DataFrame and pd.Series as supported inputs for featurize_many, the docstring for the function should reflect that.

Oops, I'll make that change.

- Finally, there seems to be different ways of checking whether something is "iterable" within a few lines of code. There is the hasattr(entries, '__getitem__') and also isinstance(entries[0], (tuple, list, np.ndarray)). I don't really like either solution - the first will consider a string as iterable, which is not something you want I think, and the second might not be a complete list. But other than something like "isinstance(entries, collections.Iterable) and not isinstance(entries, str)" I'm not sure what else to do. But at least it should be the same method since I think you are doing the same check.

I agree, the type checking in this class is muddled and I'm not sure the best route forward. `Iterable` isn't quite what we need, as sets are Iterable but lack the `__getitem__` needed later on. `dict` satisfies both, but is not suitable for use in `featurize_many` because it is does not have order. Maybe I'll just stick with `isinstance(..., ('list', 'tuple', 'ndarray', 'Series', 'DataFrame'))` and specify those are the accepted "list-like" objects in the docstring.

The current code (and documentation) is very clear that the transcript testing ignores whitespace in the comparison. The documentation states you can use regular expressions, but says 'they should not include any whitespace expressions.'

The code does (almost) exactly what the documentation says, but there are many non-obvious implications that even a user with lots of experience with regular expressions may not grasp. 

The code uses MULTILINE regular expression matching, which means the special character '$' matches 'the end of the string or just before the newline at the end of the string, and in MULTILINE mode also matches before a newline.' Newlines are whitespace, and are removed before matching the regular expression, which means you can't write a transcript with a regular expression anchored to the end of each line of output.

Same story with the '^', which matches 'the start of the string, and in MULTILINE mode also matches immediately after each newline.' You can't write a transcript that checks for a regular expression at the beginning of each line of output.

Many regular expressions try and look for words in the output. Based on the documentation you would (rightly) think that your regular expressions should avoid the special sequence '\s' which checks for any whitespace character. However, you might not realize that you should also avoid '\w', the special sequence which matches Unicode word characters. You might use a regex like '\w+' intending to match a single word. When the code removes the whitespace, '\w+' will likely end up matching the entire result, across multiple lines of output. Probably not what you intended.

Given these many non-intuitive implications and behaviors, **I recommend we change the behavior of the transcript testing feature to not do anything special with whitespace**. This is a breaking change, and I don't recommend it lightly.

However, I'm a regular expression guy, and while trying to figure this all out, I wrote thirteen transcripts which included regular expressions. Only 2 of the thirteen worked as I expected, even when I had in mind the warning from the documentation to 'not include any whitespace expressions'.

If your subclass of Cmd2 has commands that return static and predictable results, then the current behavior probably suits you just fine. For any other usage, the current transcript behavior regarding regular expressions requires more patience than I believe we can rightfully expect from anyone.

I was a little confused, because I seemed to remember that Django already has exactly that sort of warning. And in fact it does: [`TypeError('"reply_to" argument must be a list or tuple')`](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.8.17/django/core/mail/message.py#L246-L247). But Django checks it in`EmailMessage.__init__`, so it only catches the problem if you give `reply_to` as a param when you create the EmailMessage -- not if you set the attribute later like in your example.

It'd probably be helpful for Anymail to repeat Django's "expects list of strings, not a bare string" tests at send time, to catch the case you describe. 

Even though it would be a nicer API, I'd rather not automatically convert a bare string to a list. Django core defines the behavior of those attributes, and if Anymail handled them differently it would complicate switching between EmailBackends.

(Note to self: also catch bare lazy strings in to/cc/cc/reply_to; Django's current tests are probably fooled by that case. And the resulting error probably ends up [even more cryptic](https://github.com/anymail/django-anymail/issues/34).)

I'm a bit skeptical about the spun-off `char` caster. The implementation adds `make_caster_type` as a gatekeeper for all `type_casters` with a specialization and SFIANE which will need to be evaluated for every single caster, but it'll only ever be useful for a remote corner case. Python doesn't even know about single character types and any API which implements moving single characters across language boundaries is going to be slow (to put it mildly) and most likely bad design. So why not leave the simpler unified caster and just acknowledge the rare corner case in the docs?

There are still issues with your compilation; given the frenetic nature of your original post and solution, I'm a bit concerned that you're rushing this through rather than thinking carefully about how you're building things, and I urge you to slow down a little to save yourself time and effort in the future.

Some things to think about:
- it is definitely wrong to set `-DPYTHON_VERSION=2.7 -DPYPY_VERSION=1 -DWITH_THREAD`.  These *must* be set by the appropriate Python headers; if they aren't being set properly, there's something wrong with your include paths that specifying them in the compilation *cannot* fix.  Overriding them like you do *will* break something sooner or later.
- Python 2.7 .so modules in general shouldn't link to libpython2.7, and PyPy .so modules in general shouldn't link to libpypy-c.  PyPy modules *definitely* shouldn't link to *both* libpypy-c *and* libpython2.7.
- You would save yourself a good chunk of cmake code if you used the cmake scripts included and installed with pybind.  Those scripts are designed to get all the linking and flags correct.

If certbot is trying to serve a cert for a randomly generated name then this will not work on an Apache machine which is behind HAProxy redirection as HAProxy will have no knowledge of where to direct the random request - it doesn't even look as the request is coming through with SNI info and so, at least in my instance, HAProxy is dumping it through to the website which is it's default.

In order for me to get around this I just have to change my HAProxy whilst doing the update, but this then prevents me doing automatic updates without doing some magic to coordinate reconfiguring HAProxy as part of the process.

To further explain as well as give a concrete example, if Certbot depends on `python-cryptography` and someone wants to upload a new version of `python-cryptography`, it is uploaded and released in the Debian repositories without checking that the new version of `python-cryptography` works with Certbot. If a user upgrades their system (or installs Certbot for the first time) they will get this new version of `python-crypytography` and the old version is removed if it was installed.

If we pinned an older version of cryptography, Certbot is broken because it will immediately crash saying the required version of `python-cryptography` isn't available. If we don't pin, Certbot will try to work with the new version and error out as normal if a problem occurs.

More problematically, the attempt to restore `account`, `server`, and `staging` variables is ineffectual because we've already called `_init_le_client` by the time this happens. A band-aid for that could be to push that call down the stack and make it inside `_auth_from_available`, right before we first call methods on `le_client`. We would then pass the client back up as an additional return variable.

A cleaner solution would be to split `_auth_from_available` into a "figure out what we're doing" function and then an "actually do stuff" function, with the client being initialised in between.  I'm going to take a look at @ohemorange's refactor to see if that's already in the works :)

# Amended process

## Common
Fetch latest data, migrate them, attach claimed zones and reindex
```bash
wget https://www.data.gouv.fr/s/resources/geozones/20170615-160459/geozones-msgpack.tar.xz
wget https://www.data.gouv.fr/s/resources/geologos/20170615-161205/geologos.tar.xz
udata spatial load geozones-msgpack.tar.xz  --drop
udata territories load_logos  geologos.tar.xz
udata db migrate
udata territories migrate_zones_ids
udata organizations attach_zone fr:commune:83098@1942-01-01 ville-du-pradet
udata search init -df
```

## Local
Edit your `udata.cfg` to set:
```python
HANDLED_LEVELS = ('fr:commune', 'fr:departement', 'fr:region')
```

## On Data.gouv.fr

the `get_raw/set_raw` one comes from a confusion with `Parameter` and `_BaseParameter` classes - in the first, you have to redefine `get/set` methods, while in the second you have to redefine `get_raw/set_raw`; but because constructor of `Parameter` calls the constructor of the `_BaseParameter` before dealing with `get/set` methods, you *can* redefine `get_raw/set_raw` even though you are inheriting from `Parameter`. But this is not your fault, we will clean up `Parameter` and `_BaseParameter` classes in some time in the future.

And the "`_multiplier` is not callable" can be solved by first getting it into a local variable like `multiplier_param = self._multiplier`, and then calling the new variable. Extra type annotations can help with making this code understandable.

Regarding Callbacks vs SessionRunHooks:
> Why aren't Callback and tf.train.SessionRunHook compatible?

They are compatible: you can convert between them with http://tensorpack.readthedocs.io/modules/callbacks.html#tensorpack.callbacks.HookToCallback .

Tensorpack callbacks exist mainly because it is written long before `SessionRunHook` ever exists.
It has one small extra feature about scheduling -- it has the concept of epoch which allows you to do something once a while. But you can easily implement this with SessionRunHook as well. 

Regarding trainer vs tf.estimator

I personally think `tf.Estimator` is not well designed and therefore not flexible enough. But I also think it does have something that tensorpack trainers can learn from.

Also, `tf.Estimator` does not support efficient data-parallel training, until very recently (still in contrib). However tensorpack trainer supports it from the very beginning.

@Tinche: Oh, hello! Thanks for the offer! If you want to work on this then it'd be much appreciated for sure. Not having a good pytest plugin is pretty embarrassing for a project that prides itself on testing :-).

I'm not sure how much of the details of pytest-asyncio will carry over, because trio doesn't have the concept of an explicit loop object. OTOH trio *does* consider testing to be a first-class consideration, so it provides a [`@trio.testing.trio_test` decorator](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/12c41b4ede8cae2034f19ebe6d12b3d7e63c8c55/trio/testing.py#L33-L53) out-of-the-box that's suitable to use with any testing framework (currently a bit rudimentary but it'd be nice to improve it further).

I was imagining that a v0.0.1 version of pytest-trio would basically just be [this code](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/12c41b4ede8cae2034f19ebe6d12b3d7e63c8c55/trio/tests/conftest.py#L16-L27) pulled out into a standalone plugin, plus some configuration options added. (Probably: require that either a pytest.ini config variable be set to enable it globally, or else a `pytest.mark.trio` be applied, the latter intended for projects that might have some tests that use trio, some that use asyncio, etc.)

That would already be enough to let trio start dogfooding the package ourselves, and also be super helpful to others...

Then fancier things would be fixture support -- this is a bit tricky without explicit loop support, because you have to delay the actual fixture initialization until you start executing the test, but I think it's doable -- timeouts, helpers to detect things like ResourceWarning and "coroutine was never awaited", [and so forth...](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/labels/pytest-trio%20relevant)... but we can figure that out later :-). What do you think?

@yanzixiang by the way, thanks for the link to pyserial-asyncio, I hadn't seen that one before. I just looked at its source code, and I think asyncserial is probably a better library to look at for inspiration. Pyserial-asyncio uses asyncio's complicated and low-level transport/protocol system, while asyncserial uses a much simpler stream API that's similar to trio's.

> a function like run has to take the thing to run, and it also has to take its own arguments to configure how it runs it.

Here's another option I don't see listed. What about not supporting passing configuration options directly to `run()` at all? Instead, if non-default behavior is desired, the caller can instantiate, say, a `TrioRunner` object with those options and use `runner.run()`. It looks like you're [almost doing that here anyways](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/aee2676fd5096daf2849db9756242a30e0aa1f30/trio/_core/_run.py#L1210), but with a `Runner` class that has a slightly different responsibility, and is perhaps not exposed as part of the public API.

There's something to be said for a clean function signature that doesn't mix arguments for `async_fn` with arguments for `run`. It also provides more options for configuring `run()` and may age better, too, because you won't have to add more and more kwargs over time. (One of them is already called `restrict_keyboard_interrupt_to_checkpoints` :), which might be better as an attribute.)

Whoops, just realized there's a bug here: on Windows, we store and look up sockets by Python object id, not by fileno, so if one task does `wait_socket_readable(sockobj)` and another does `notify_socket_closed(sockobj.fileno())`, trio won't realize that those are the same socket. We should be like the Unix versions of these functions, and always use the `.fileno()` as the lookup key.

> IMHO the cancelled task is … well, cancelled. It's finished. Its job is no longer to wait on anything

In general, Trio's principle is that once a function returns, then it's done, it's not running anymore. (With the narrow exception of functions where you explicitly pass in a nursery.) This is arguably the *most* foundational of Trio's design principles.

Following this principle, when you cancel any of trio's built-in operations, then they wait for the cancellation to complete before raising `Cancelled`. `Cancelled` means it's actually cancelled, not scheduled to be cancelled at some future point. In most cases that arise inside trio itself, this is pretty straightforward, because cancellation can be done synchronously. But this is why `run_sync_in_worker_thread` ignores cancellation by default (unless you specifically tell it that the operation is OK to abandon, which is a hack to make `getaddrinfo` work the way you expect – since `getaddrinfo` is totally side-effect-free it's impossible to tell whether it's still running after being cancelled). And if we use IOCP on Windows, then operations like `socket.send` can have asynchronous cancellation (you have to submit a request to the kernel to cancel the operation, and then later you find out whether it was actually cancelled); we would definitely not want to request the cancellation and then immediately raise `Cancelled`. (The desire to support stuff like this is part of why `wait_task_rescheduled` has such a complicated calling convention, with the argument that's a callback that takes an argument that's a callback.)

I can imagine that there are specific circumstances where people are using RPC systems in ways where they don't want these semantics, or want to relax them like we do for `getaddrinfo`. But we should certainly take the standard wait-for-operations-to-resolve-before-returning semantics into account when coming up with general recommendations.

Thanks Jerome, I've been trying to think of a clever way to do this but can't see a way around it. When converting object arrays to lists there is an ambiguity, without also knowing the shape it's not possible to correctly restore the original array. Just to illustrate for the record:

```python
In [21]: a = np.empty((2, 2), dtype=object)

In [22]: a[0, 0] = 0

In [23]: a[0, 1] = 1

In [24]: a[1, 0] = 2

In [25]: a[1, 1] = 3

In [26]: a
Out[26]: 
array([[0, 1],
       [2, 3]], dtype=object)

In [27]: a.tolist()
Out[27]: [[0, 1], [2, 3]]

In [35]: b = np.empty(2, dtype=object)

In [36]: b[0] = [0, 1]

In [37]: b[1] = [2, 3]

In [38]: b
Out[38]: array([list([0, 1]), list([2, 3])], dtype=object)

In [39]: b.tolist()
Out[39]: [[0, 1], [2, 3]]
```

Your solution to append the shape to the list of items to be encoded seems good to me.

To take this forward it would be good to add some unit tests that expose this problem in the JSON and MsgPack codecs. It should be doable with some manually crafted arrays like above, i.e., the array `b` when passed through encode() and back through decode() will end up with a different shape and size. 

The next thing to figure out is how to handle data format compatibility as gracefully as possible. Might be worth giving this some discussion, to work through the consequences.

The [rule I set for numcodecs](http://numcodecs.readthedocs.io/en/stable/contributing.html#data-format-compatibility) to maintain data format compatibility is that the encoding format associated with a given codec ID should never change. Codec IDs are associated with codec classes via the codec_id property, e.g., [here](https://github.com/zarr-developers/numcodecs/blob/master/numcodecs/json.py#L33). The codec ID is used by Zarr in the array metadata, and is the key used by [numcodecs' registry](http://numcodecs.readthedocs.io/en/stable/registry.html) which Zarr uses to look up an implementation for a given codec. So if we change the codec format to append the shape, we have to also modify the codec IDs, as these are effectively new codecs. I had thought for cases like this then we could change the codec IDs to something like "json2" and "msgpack2". So, i.e., the numcodecs library would provide implementations of new codecs "json2" and "msgpack2" which store the array shape in the encoded data, as well as continuing to provide implementations of "json" and "msgpack" codecs so old data that doesn't encounter this problem could still be read, although we would warn users not to use "json" or "msgpack" somehow because these are broken for some cases. This is possibly a little ugly, but preserves format compatibility.

There is another approach we could consider here, because we know that the json and msgpack encodings are basically broken. We could change the implementation of "json" and "msgpack" codecs as proposed, but keep the codec ID unchanged. This would break data format compatibility in the backwards direction, in the sense that older versions of numcodecs would not be able to read data created by newer versions of the library with the "json" or "msgpack" codec. To avoid breaking compatibility in the forwards direction, i.e., to allow newer versions of numcodecs to continue reading data created by older versions, we could add some conditional logic inside decode() to check the type of the last item in the encoded list. If it's a string then you know the shape is missing, and attempt to process using the old logic. If it's not a string, assume it's the shape and process with the new logic. This breaks the codec ID rule, but may be better from the point of view of API simplicity (i.e., we can just keep a single implementation of JSON codec).

Both solutions have pros and cons, would be interested to hear views.
